data.title: Wall Street jumps as Trump says he won’t impose Greenland tariffs on Feb. 1
data.description:
The markets (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) responded with elevated enthusiasm on Wednesday’s afternoon trade after U.S. President Donald Trump announced on Truth Social that he will not be imposing the tariffs scheduled to go into effect Feb. 1.

“Based upon a very productive meeting that I have had with the Secretary General of NATO, Mark Rutte, we have formed the framework of a future deal with respect to Greenland and, in fact, the entire Arctic Region,” Trump said.

“Based upon this understanding, I will not be imposing the Tariffs that were scheduled to go into effect on February 1st. Additional discussions are being held concerning The Golden Dome as it pertains to Greenland,” he added.

Earlier on Wednesday, European leaders announced they had suspended a trade agreement with the U.S. as Trump continued his push to acquire Greenland during a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was last 1.5% on afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was 1.5% as well, and the Dow (DJI) was 1.6%.

Investors can follow more on Wednesday’s market moves here.

data.title: F.N.B. outlines mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth for 2026 as balance sheet surpasses $50B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: F.N.B. Corporation (FNB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Vincent J. Delie, Chairman, President & CEO, stated that FNB reported fourth quarter operating net income available to common shareholders of $182 million or $0.50 per diluted common share, with full year operating net income of $577 million and operating earnings per diluted share of $1.59. Delie highlighted, "Full year operating EPS grew 14% year-over-year, driven by the 9% growth in net interest income, significant margin expansion and record noninterest income." He emphasized the company's focus on resetting the balance sheet, managing loan concentrations, and improving the loan-to-deposit ratio to 89.7%. Delie also announced the transfer of approximately $200 million of performing residual mortgage loans to held for sale, expected to close in Q1 2026.

  • Delie highlighted the digital strategy: "Our comprehensive digital strategy, including our early adoption of AI, remains a driving force behind client acquisition, engagement and convenience." The introduction of a payment switch feature and plans for additional digital capabilities were discussed as part of FNB's Clicks-to-Bricks strategy.

  • Delie noted, "Through our disciplined expense management culture, FNB has achieved annual cost savings of $10 million to $20 million per year since 2019," and projected even higher cost savings in 2026 through automation and process improvements.

  • Gary L. Guerrieri, Chief Credit Officer, reported strong asset quality: "Total delinquency ended the quarter at 71 basis points, up 6 bps from the prior quarter with NPLs and OREO down 6 bps, ending at a multiyear low of 31 basis points." He added, "Net charge-offs totaled 19 basis points and 20 basis points for the year, showing continued strong performance throughout an uncertain economic environment."

  • Vincent J. Calabrese, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Fourth quarter operating net income totaled a record $181.8 million or $0.50 per share when excluding a discretionary $20 million charitable contribution to the FNB Foundation partially offset by a reduction in the estimated FDIC special assessment." He also said, "Record total revenues of nearly $458 million, grew a very strong 12.4% on an operating basis and operating pre-provision net revenue grew 21.5% from the year ago quarter."

Outlook

  • Calabrese provided 2026 guidance: "For full year 2026, period-end loans and deposits are expected to grow mid-single digits versus year-end 2025 as we continue to increase our market share across our diverse geographic footprint." Net interest income for 2026 is expected to be between $1.495 billion and $1.535 billion, with Q1 net interest income projected between $355 million and $365 million. Noninterest income is guided to be between $370 million and $390 million for the year, and noninterest expense between $1 billion and $1.02 billion. Provision expense is expected between $85 million and $105 million for 2026.

  • Delie reiterated, "Looking ahead to 2026, we are confident in our ability to deliver meaningful loan and deposit growth, margin expansion and further diversification of fee income."

Financial Results

  • FNB's total assets at year-end 2025 exceeded $50 billion for the first time. Fourth quarter average loans and leases were $35 billion, up $169 million from last quarter. Average deposits were $38.6 billion, an increase of $740 million linked quarter annualized.

  • Net interest income for Q4 was $365.4 million, up 1.7% from the prior quarter. Noninterest income reached $92.3 million, with wealth management revenues up 15% year-over-year. The net interest margin was 3.28%, up 3 basis points from Q3.

  • Operating noninterest expense totaled $256.5 million, with an efficiency ratio of 53.8%, 307 basis points better than Q4 2024.

  • FNB's capital levels remained at record levels with a CET1 ratio at 11.4% and tangible common equity ratio at 8.9%.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about fee income guidance for 2026 and drivers for achieving upper-end guidance. Calabrese said, "We had all-time highs for 7 of our fee-based businesses for the full year and 4 of them in the fourth quarter alone... The newer businesses that we've talked about starting to contribute at higher levels is definitely baked into the guidance."

  • Tamayo followed up on operating leverage. Calabrese noted, "Our guidance includes a meaningful increase in PPNR and in the operating leverage... expenses growing in the low single digits, while we're continuing to invest in the new initiatives."

  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Inquired about loan growth, particularly in C&I and CRE. Delie responded, "The C&I production was extraordinarily good... we used '25 to kind of position our company to grow CRE loans and to grow C&I loans more rapidly."

  • Gunther also asked about CET1 targets. Calabrese: "Dividend payout for the full year, then you combine that with our expectation for strong internal capital generation... we're in the best position we've ever been to deploy capital to optimize shareholder value."

  • Casey Haire: Asked about deposit beta trends and credit provision outlook. Calabrese: "I would say 30% or so by the end of the year... still kind of a mid-30s once this cycle finishes." Guerrieri confirmed provision guidance assumptions.

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about buyback price sensitivity and M&A. Calabrese: "We think our stock is worth a lot more than where it's trading... there's not a bright line where we would stop."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for specifics on loan growth, capital deployment, and efficiency ratios, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions focused on the sustainability of performance and potential for increased capital returns.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing strong momentum, strategic digital investments, and readiness for growth. Delie said, "We are very pleased with our financial results and achieved a number of records for 2025 including revenue, noninterest income and EPS."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained focused on growth and efficiency, while management's confidence appeared even more pronounced, especially regarding digital initiatives and capital flexibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for loan and deposit growth shifted from projecting toward the lower end of mid-single digits in Q3 to a firm mid-single-digit expectation for 2026.

  • Management's tone increased in confidence around technology deployment, cost savings, and operating leverage, while analysts continued to focus on capital returns and loan growth.

  • Key metrics such as CET1 ratio, tangible book value, and net interest margin all improved quarter-over-quarter.

  • Strategic focus shifted further toward digital banking, AI, and automation as drivers of efficiency and growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Guerrieri noted ongoing monitoring of line utilization and industry concentrations, but reported continued positive credit migration and no material impacts from tariffs since Q1.

  • Delinquency and NPLs remained low, and management emphasized strong credit risk practices and capital strength as mitigation strategies.

  • Analysts questioned loan pricing competitiveness and the sustainability of efficiency gains, with management stressing discipline and selectivity in lending and expense management.

Final Takeaway

FNB closed 2025 with record revenue, strong profitability, and a balance sheet exceeding $50 billion in assets. Management highlighted a robust outlook for 2026, projecting mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth, further margin expansion, and continued investment in digital capabilities and automation. The company’s capital strength and disciplined credit management underpin its confidence in pursuing growth opportunities while maintaining operational efficiency and flexibility for shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: MRNA is S&P 500’s top performing stock, here are the best Quant pharma companies
data.description:
The pharmaceutical sector continues to attract investor attention, with recent clinical developments highlighting the industry’s innovation potential.

Moderna (MRNA) emerged as the top-performing pharmaceutical stock in the S&P 500 (SP500) on Wednesday after announcing that its mRNA-based cancer therapy, intismeran autogene, combined with Merck’s (MRK) Keytruda, demonstrated a 49% reduction in the risk of tumor recurrence or death over five years in a late-stage melanoma trial.

The following stock screen focuses on companies categorized within the pharmaceuticals sector (PJP), (XPH), (PPH). The ranking is determined by Seeking Alpha’s advanced Quant Rating system, which evaluates stocks based on multiple quantitative factors designed to predict future performance potential.

The Quant Rating system categorizes stocks across a spectrum from “Strong Buy” to “Hold” based on various metrics. Stocks receiving a Strong Buy rating generally score above 4.5, while Buy ratings fall between approximately 3.5 and 4.5, and Hold ratings typically range from around 3.0 to 3.5. The stocks presented below represent the highest-rated pharmaceutical companies according to these quantitative metrics.

Here are the top pharma stocks by Quant ratings. Interestingly, Moderna (MRNA) is not among the top 15:

  1. Indivior PLC (INDV) - SA Quant rating: 4.94 (Strong Buy) (YTD: -6.42%)
  2. Liquidia Corporation (LQDA) - SA Quant rating: 4.93 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 13.99%)
  3. Sol-Gel Technologies Ltd. (SLGL) - SA Quant rating: 4.93 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 71.02%)
  4. Pfizer Inc. (PFE) - SA Quant rating: 4.89 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 1.31%)
  5. Oramed Pharmaceuticals Inc. (ORMP) - SA Quant rating: 4.86 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 20.14%)
  6. Astellas Pharma Inc. (ALPMY) - SA Quant rating: 4.86 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 4.01%)
  7. Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) - SA Quant rating: 4.82 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 8.27%)
  8. Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) - SA Quant rating: 4.73 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 0.06%)
  9. Innoviva, Inc. (INVA) - SA Quant rating: 4.68 (Strong Buy) (YTD: -2.20%)
  10. Amneal Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (AMRX) - SA Quant rating: 4.50 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 6.05%)
  11. Theravance Biopharma, Inc. (TBPH) - SA Quant rating: 4.30 (Buy) (YTD: 8.67%)
  12. ANI Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ANIP) - SA Quant rating: 4.14 (Buy) (YTD: 3.52%)
  13. Septerna, Inc. (SEPN) - SA Quant rating: 4.06 (Buy) (YTD: -8.62%)
  14. Phibro Animal Health Corporation (PAHC) - SA Quant rating: 4.04 (Buy) (YTD: 10.28%)
  15. Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR) - SA Quant rating: 3.93 (Buy) (YTD: -22.65%)

data.title: Apple's weakness is a 'buying opportunity' into earnings: Goldman
data.description:
The recent weakness in Apple's (AAPL) shares — down seven weeks in a row — is a “buying opportunity” ahead of the iPhone creator's quarterly earnings, Goldman Sachs said.

Goldman Sachs analyst Michael Ng said he expects Apple to earn $2.66 per share for the coming quarter, with iPhone revenue estimated to grow 13% year-over-year and shipments up 5% over the same time frame, including 26% growth in China. Ng has a Buy rating and a $320 price target on Apple.

“iPhone demand in the next 2 years is likely to benefit from the iPhone Fold (Fall 2026, 4.5/25.4M units in F2026/F2027), the shift to a biannual iPhone launch cycle with the iPhone 18 base and iPhone Air 2 delayed from Fall 2026 to Spring 2027, and new software upgrades with iOS and Siri 2.0,” Ng wrote in a note to clients. “Although App Store spending decelerated again in F1Q26E to +7% [year-over-year], Services revenue growth (GSe: +14% [year-over-year]) should be supported by momentum in other categories (e.g., TAC, iCloud+, AppleCare+) with further tailwinds in F2026 from new ad formats in the App Store.”

Ng added the price and mix growth of Apple's products and the continued shift towards services should help boost gross margins, even if memory costs rise. Additionally, the company's partnership with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) for Siri and continued iPhone demand ”should demonstrate to the market that the iPhone will remain the consumer device of choice for accessing new AI tools, clearing overhangs related to competition.”

data.title: Realty Income slides after six-session rally
data.description:
Realty Income (O) shares fell 0.41% to $61.37 on Wednesday, ending a six-session winning streak.

The stock had gained about 4.64% between January 12 and January 20, far outpacing the S&P 500 that fell -2.59% over the same period.

U.S. real estate stocks saw mixed signals, after earlier gains driven by falling mortgage rates and a sharp rise in loan applications were offset by fresh policy and macro concerns. Commentary from President Donald Trump on housing policy, including efforts to address institutional home purchases and bolster affordability, added to positive sentiment for the industry.

That policy uncertainty came alongside weaker housing data, after pending home sales plunged far more than expected, adding to doubts about the near-term health of the housing market. Broader market risk-off pressure, with U.S. stock futures and major indices under strain, also weighed on real estate names, helping explain why Realty Income slipped even as parts of the sector had earlier shown gains.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, O is rated with a Buy, with grades of A in growth and profitability, a B in momentum, while a C in revisions and a C+ in terms of valuation.

SA analysts suggest buying the stock.

An analyst said Realty Income remains attractive for income-focused investors, citing its resilient cash flows and valuation. The analyst said the REIT “offers bond-like stability, high occupancy, and resilient AFFO growth,” adding that the stock is trading “at a discount” to its historical valuation, with forward AFFO growth projected at about 3% annually.

However, turning to the Wall Street, analysts are cautious, with seven of 25 analysts rating the stock with a Buy or higher, 17 recommending a hold, while one suggests a sell.

The stock remains up 9.14% year-to-date and has surged 9.32% higher over the past month.

data.title: OpenAI unveils program to help countries future-proof education, workforces with AI
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) unveiled a new program on Wednesday to help countries future-proof their education and workforces, utilizing artificial intelligence. 

Known as OpenAI’s Education for Countries, the new program will bring the ChatGPT maker closer to governments around the world and universities to use AI in several ways: personalizing learning, cutting administrative burden, and preparing students for the workforce, the company said. 

“Studies⁠ project that by 2030 nearly 40% of the core skills workers rely on today will change, driven largely by AI,” OpenAI said on its website. “By embedding AI tools, training, and research into the core infrastructure of schools and universities, education systems can evolve alongside these shifts and better prepare students to thrive in a world with AI.”

There will be four “core elements” to the program, OpenAI said — AI tools for learning; learning outcomes research; OpenAI Certifications and training; and a “global network” of partners. As part of the program, OpenAI said it will provide access to ChatGPT Edu, its GPT-5.2 model, among other aids.

The first countries to get access to the program include Estonia, Greece, and the United Arab Emirates, among others. OpenAI added that the next group of countries will be announced later this year.

data.title: Truist signals 4–5% revenue growth and $4B share repurchase for 2026 while targeting 15% ROTCE in 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Rogers highlighted that Truist closed 2025 with "strong results and clear momentum heading into 2026," emphasizing net income available to common shareholders of $1.3 billion or $1 per diluted share for the fourth quarter and $5 billion or $3.82 per diluted share for the full year. Rogers noted, "These results include certain charges such as severance and an accrual related to a specific legal matter that was settled in the first quarter of 2026, which totaled $0.12 a share for the quarter and $0.18 per share for the year."

  • Rogers underscored Truist's strategic priorities, including "accelerate revenue growth, drive greater positive operating leverage, continue to invest while maintaining our expense and risk discipline and return capital to shareholders at an accelerated rate." He signaled confidence in delivering "revenue growth at least twice the pace of 2025, greater positive operating leverage, higher levels of capital return and improved profitability."

  • CEO Rogers announced plans to "open 100 new insight-driven branches in high-growth markets as well as enhancements to more than 300 branch locations in all markets."

  • CFO Michael Maguire provided detail on recent changes in earnings presentation, specifying, "Effective December 31, 2025, we changed the reporting line labeled card and payment fees to a new reporting line called card and treasury management fees... We believe these changes more accurately reflect how we're managing our business and will give investors more insights into how we're progressing with important fee income-generating initiatives."

  • Maguire also stated, "During the quarter, we repurchased $750 million of common stock and announced a new share repurchase authorization of up to $10 billion with no expiration date."

Outlook

  • Maguire projected, "For full year 2026, we expect revenue to increase 4% to 5% relative to 2025 revenue of $20.5 billion, driven by 3% to 4% growth in net interest income and mid- to high single-digit growth in noninterest income."

  • The company expects "full year 2026 GAAP noninterest expense to increase by 1.25% to 2.25% in '26 versus GAAP '25 noninterest expense of $12.1 billion" and "positive operating leverage of 275 basis points in 2026."

  • Maguire detailed that "we expect net charge-offs of about 55 basis points in 2026, which is relatively stable compared with net charge-offs of 54 basis points in 2025."

  • Truist is targeting "approximately $4 billion of share repurchases during the year."

  • For Q1 2026, Maguire indicated, "we expect revenue to decrease approximately 2% to 3% relative to fourth quarter revenue of $5.3 billion."

Financial Results

  • Truist reported fourth quarter 2025 net income available to common shareholders of $1.3 billion or $1 per diluted share, and for the full year, $5 billion or $3.82 per diluted share. The quarter included a $130 million charge due to a legal settlement and $0.04 per share in severance charges.

  • Revenue increased 1.1% from the previous quarter, supported by 1.9% growth in net interest income.

  • Average loans held for investment increased $4.3 billion or 1.3% quarter-over-quarter to $325 billion. Full year average loans rose 3.6% to $316 billion, driven by 5.4% growth in average consumer and card loans and 2.4% growth in average commercial loans.

  • Average deposits remained stable sequentially, with a noted improvement in mix as higher-cost broker deposits declined and lower-cost client deposits increased. The average interest-bearing deposit cost dropped 27 basis points to 2.23%.

  • Net interest margin increased 6 basis points to 3.07%. For 2026, net interest income is expected to grow by 3% to 4%.

  • Noninterest income decreased $12 million or 0.8% from the third quarter, primarily due to lower fees in several categories, offset by higher investment banking and trading income.

  • Noninterest expense increased 5.2% quarter-over-quarter, mainly from the legal accrual and higher personnel costs. Excluding these, noninterest expense fell 0.3% from the previous quarter.

  • Asset quality remained strong, with net charge-offs at 57 basis points and nonperforming loans stable at 48 basis points of total loans.

Q&A

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about loan growth guidance versus the 8% year-over-year exit rate and the focus between commercial and consumer. Rogers responded that "C&I is -- had its sort of strongest quarter. I mean, production was up really, really significantly and just high-quality business... I think the result of that is going to be a little more wholesale."

  • Nash followed up on net interest margin trajectory. Maguire answered, "while we sort of enter the year at 3.07%, we would expect to back up just a touch. But throughout the course of the rest of the year, we would expect to see margin expansion, especially in the second half."

  • John Pancari, Evercore: Queried on 2027 ROTCE target. Rogers said, "So think about it maybe in its simplest term as the concept of holding the denominator of capital and dollars steady and then improving momentum and return from the numerator."

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Asked about capital markets outlook. Rogers stated, "We have really good momentum coming out of the second half of the year and quite frankly, on a lot of cylinders... this is a business I feel really confident in."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth, capital returns, and the sustainability of strong results, with a tone that was inquisitive and occasionally pressing, especially regarding the pace of loan growth, NIM, and future capital returns.

  • Management maintained a consistently confident and optimistic tone, frequently referencing "momentum" and "confidence," with statements such as "I am confident in our future."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was even more assertive regarding strategic direction and capital return acceleration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced more specific guidance for 2026, including 4% to 5% revenue growth and $4 billion in share repurchases, compared to broader goals in the prior quarter.

  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to strong confidence, with clear commitments to doubling revenue growth and achieving 15% ROTCE in 2027.

  • Analysts’ focus shifted somewhat from concerns about credit quality and portfolio composition in the previous quarter to questions about capital deployment, efficiency, and growth mix in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic risk, specifically employment levels, as a primary concern for future performance.

  • Maguire noted ongoing competitive risks and the challenge of tight credit spreads.

  • Analysts probed about potential headwinds in loan and deposit growth, capital markets volatility, and the sustainability of cost controls.

Final Takeaway

Truist closed 2025 with strong financial results, robust loan and deposit growth, and a clear plan for 2026 focused on accelerating revenue, operating leverage, and capital return. Management projects a 4% to 5% revenue increase, $4 billion in share repurchases, and remains confident in achieving a 15% ROTCE in 2027, while highlighting investments in technology, talent, and branch expansion as key drivers for future growth and profitability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Citizens Financial stock climbs after Q4 earnings top consensus on NII, fees
data.description:
Citizens Financial Group (CFG) shares gained 6% in Wednesday afternoon trading after the regional bank posted a double-beat on Q4 2025 earnings and revenue, reflecting strong fee growth and an improvement in net interest margin.

Q4 underlying EPS of $1.13, topping the $1.11 average analyst estimate, rose from $1.05 in Q3 and $0.83 a year ago.

Q4 total revenue of $2.16B, vs. $2.15B consensus, increased from $2.12B in Q3 and $1.99B in Q4 2024.

Net interest income came in at $1.54B, up from $1.49B in the earlier quarter and $1.41B in 2024's Q4.

Provision for credit losses fell to $137M from $154M in Q3 and $162M in Q4 2024.

Loans and leases totaled $142.7B in Q4 vs. $140.9B in Q3. Deposits of $183.3B vs. $180.0B in Q3.

Net interest margin (fully taxable equivalent) was 3.07 in Q4, up from 3.00 in Q3 and 2.87 in Q4 2024.

data.title: Wintrust targets mid- to high single-digit loan growth for 2026 while maintaining stable margins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Wintrust Financial Corporation (WTFC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Timothy Crane highlighted that Wintrust delivered record net income for 2025, reporting $824 million, up 19% from 2024, and emphasized "our efforts drove record net income for the year." Crane noted total assets surpassed $71 billion at year-end, and tangible book value increased by over $13 to nearly $89 a share. He stated, "our steady and consistent approach moved us into third position in deposit market share in the Chicago area, and we showed strong gains in both Wisconsin and West Michigan."
  • Crane pointed to solid operating leverage, stating, "net revenue was up 11.2%, 340 basis points higher than our noninterest expense," and credited ongoing investment in technology and talent. He added, "improved Net Promoter Scores that were already best-in-class in both retail and commercial banking in 2025."
  • COO David Dykstra said, "deposit growth was right at $1 billion during the quarter, representing a 7% increase over the prior quarter on an annualized basis. This deposit growth helped to fund continued strong fourth quarter loan growth of a similar $1.0 billion amount that represented 8% growth on an annualized basis." Dykstra also reported, "net interest income also reached another high record quarterly amount," with a $16.9 million increase over the prior quarter.
  • Vice Chairman Richard Murphy explained that commercial real estate loans grew by $322 million, mortgage warehouse by $310 million, and Wintrust Life Finance by $265 million in Q4. He added, "credit performance continued to be very solid in the fourth quarter," and highlighted that charge-offs decreased to 17 basis points from 19 basis points in the prior quarter.

Outlook

  • Crane outlined 2026 targets: "We expect mid- to high single-digit loan growth funded by a similar level of deposit growth as we continue to expand share. Given the current interest rate environment and even with a few rate changes in either direction, we expect the margin to remain relatively stable around 3.5%."
  • The company expects "improved noninterest income in our wealth management and service-based fee income businesses" and is "hopeful for the mortgage market to pick up."
  • Crane cautioned, "we are mindful of the typical seasonality that can make our quarterly growth uneven, particularly in the first half of the year."

Financial Results

  • For Q4 2025, net income was $223 million, a record and up 3% from the prior quarter. Net interest margin was 3.54%, within the annual range of 3.50% to 3.56%. Dykstra stated, "noninterest income totaled $130.4 million in the fourth quarter similar to the $130.8 million recorded in the prior quarter."
  • Noninterest expenses were $384.5 million, up slightly from $380 million in the previous quarter. Dykstra noted, "increases in employees' health insurance claims, OREO expenses, travel and entertainment and various other small expense increases were offset somewhat by seasonally lower marketing costs."
  • Nonperforming loans increased slightly to $185.8 million but remained at 35 basis points of total loans. CRE NPLs decreased to 0.18% of the CRE portfolio.

Q&A

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about drivers behind mid- to high single-digit loan growth. Crane responded that "we're cautiously optimistic about what we're hearing in the local economies where we operate" and cited strong momentum in lending verticals. Murphy added, "there's enough stability in the general picture where they're willing to invest."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Asked about deposit cost decline. Crane attributed it to "our team did a really nice job moving deposits as the Fed moved" and noted the trend in DDA deposits.
  • Chris McGratty, KBW: Inquired about operational areas needing improvement. Crane said, "we'd like the mortgage business to be stronger" and discussed disciplined approach to commercial lending.
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about capital deployment. Dykstra said, "if that number starts to get to 10.5% or above, and we don't have any good acquisition opportunities and organic growth is mid- to high single digits...you would look at buybacks and then dividend increases."
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research: Sought clarity on revenue and expense growth guidance. Dykstra clarified guidance is "off of the fourth quarter run rate on both sides."
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked if loan growth outlook assumes rate drops. Crane replied, "the assumption would be a slightly improved mortgage market in line with the Mortgage Bankers Association projections, not any dramatic drop in rates."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was generally constructive but focused on clarifying guidance, capital deployment, deposit costs, and sensitivity to mortgage market changes, indicating a neutral to slightly positive sentiment.
  • Management presented a confident and disciplined outlook, repeatedly referencing solid fundamentals and operating leverage, while acknowledging areas for improvement such as mortgage banking. Crane emphasized, "we are cautiously optimistic," and Dykstra reiterated, "we think we're very neutral for a full year with the margin."
  • Compared to the prior quarter, analyst tone remained steady, and management’s confidence was consistent, though there were more specific discussions of expense management and margin stability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language for loan and deposit growth has not materially changed, remaining at mid- to high single digits, but management was more explicit in referencing seasonality and uneven quarterly growth for 2026.
  • Strategic focus remains on organic growth, stable margins, and positive operating leverage, with ongoing caution regarding the mortgage business and CRE exposure.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin stability, deposit costs, and capital allocation, similar to the previous quarter.
  • Key financial metrics, including record net income, loan, and deposit growth, showed incremental improvement. Charge-offs fell slightly, and CRE NPLs declined further.
  • Management’s tone was confident and consistent, emphasizing operational discipline and stable credit quality. Analysts’ tone remained neutral, with a focus on clarifications.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged seasonality as a factor that may "make our quarterly growth uneven, particularly in the first half of the year."
  • Crane acknowledged "our deposit costs can be a little bit higher than some of our peers" but stated comfort with this strategy for long-term client retention.
  • Ongoing attention to the mortgage business and competitive pressures in fully funded CRE loans were noted as potential headwinds.
  • Murphy reported that nonperforming loans increased slightly but characterized this as "episodic in nature."

Final Takeaway

Wintrust Financial Corporation reported record full-year and quarterly net income in 2025, supported by solid loan and deposit growth, well-managed expenses, and stable credit quality. Management remains focused on disciplined, organic expansion with mid- to high single-digit growth targets and a stable margin for 2026, while continuing to invest in the business. The company positions itself to capitalize on opportunities in core markets and specialty businesses, while acknowledging seasonality and mortgage market fluctuations as variables to monitor in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Citizens projects 10%–12% NII growth and $700M–$850M share buybacks in 2026 while launching Reimagine the Bank
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Citizens Financial Group (CFG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bruce Van Saun stated Citizens "were pleased to finish the year with another strong quarter," highlighting net interest margin expansion of 7 basis points, positive operating leverage, strong wealth and capital markets fees, and favorable credit trends.

  • Van Saun explained, "Our Private Bank finished the year with $14.5 billion in deposits, $10 billion in client assets and $7.2 billion in loans. The business was 7% accretive to pretax income in 2025, ahead of our 5% target. Importantly, we managed this business to a 25% ROE for the year." He noted the successful reduction of noncore assets and a $100 million run-rate benefit from their top 10 program.

  • Van Saun emphasized the launch of "Reimagine the Bank," a digital and operational transformation with "around 50 initiatives at the outset" intended to drive "meaningful enhancements to customer experience" and "real revenue benefits in addition to the targeted expense efficiency improvements."

  • He projected a favorable 2026 macroeconomic environment, expecting "solid GDP growth, stable unemployment and inflation falling by the end of the year," and stated, "Our focus for the foreseeable future remains on our attractive organic growth agenda."

  • CFO Aunoy Banerjee commented, "We delivered EPS of $3.86 for 2025, up 19% on an underlying basis, and that includes a $0.28 or just over 7% contribution from the Private Bank." Banerjee added, "Net interest income was up 4% as we delivered 13 basis points of margin expansion. Fees were up a strong 11% on an underlying basis, led by record results in both wealth, up 22% and Capital Markets, which had a nice pickup in the second half, up 9% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Citizens projects "NII to be up 10% to 12% with NIM expanding about 4 to 5 basis points a quarter towards 3.25% in 4Q '26. Loan growth is expected to pick up this year with spot loans up 3% to 5%, average loans up 2.5% to 3.5% and overall earning assets up 4% to 5%."

  • Noninterest income is expected to rise "6% to 8%, driven primarily by wealth and capital markets." Expense growth is projected at 4.5% with "operating leverage in excess of 500 basis points."

  • Share repurchases are envisioned at "$700 million to $850 million" for 2026, and management will target a CET1 ratio of "10.5% to 10.6%."

  • Banerjee stated, "Credit is projected to continue to improve through the year with our outlook for net charge-offs in the mid- to high 30s basis points."

  • The Reimagine the Bank initiative is expected to have "about $50 million of front-loaded onetime costs that will be effectively offset by $45 million of benefits to be realized later in the year." The program targets "fully phased-in pretax run rate benefits of approximately $450 million as we exit 2028."

Financial Results

  • Citizens reported Q4 2025 EPS of $1.13, up 8% sequentially and 36% year-on-year. Net interest income for the quarter increased 3% sequentially, with net interest margin rising to 3.07%.

  • Wealth business delivered "another record quarter," up 5% sequentially and 31% year-over-year. Capital Markets recorded its "third best quarter ever, up 16% year-over-year."

  • Expenses rose 0.6% quarter-over-quarter, with the efficiency ratio improving to 62%. Average and period-end loans were up 1% (or 2% excluding noncore runoff). Total deposits increased to $183 billion, up 2% sequentially, with noninterest-bearing deposits at 22% of the mix.

  • Credit metrics improved, with net charge-offs at 43 basis points and allowance for credit losses at 1.53%. The company returned $326 million to shareholders in Q4 through dividends and buybacks.

  • The Private Bank contributed $0.10 to Q4 EPS and ended the year at $14.5 billion in deposits and $7.2 billion in loans. Tangible book value per share increased to $38.07, up 4% sequentially and 18% year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the flow-through from the Reimagine the Bank program, and whether benefits would be reinvested or realized in the bottom line. Van Saun replied, "It's still a bit of an open question as to how much of that flows through... If we want to invest new dollars, try to figure out where to pinch the expense base to self-fund that. So I would expect that the flow-through should be high, but we're not going to make that call at this point."

  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Inquired about loan growth guidance and balance sheet optimization. Van Saun highlighted "idiosyncratic growth driver in the Private Bank" and noted noncore runoff had lessened, with commercial and consumer loan growth supported by new products and regions.

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Questioned fee growth assumptions and Capital Markets outlook. Van Saun acknowledged, "We had a very strong fee year in 2025," and described the 6%–8% fee growth guide as cautious due to potential market uncertainties.

  • Multiple analysts probed deposit growth, efficiency ratio targets, and details about Private Bank loan categories, with management emphasizing broad-based growth, deposit stability, and continued investments in the Private Bank.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive but pressed for granularity on expense management, Reimagine the Bank benefits, and the sustainability of loan and fee growth. Questions were direct and showed confidence in Citizens’ outlook, while expressing some caution around execution of large initiatives.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing successful execution and strong market positioning. Phrases such as "We are confident..." and "We expect..." appeared throughout. There was some measured caution, particularly on the timing and flow-through of cost savings.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained upbeat, with additional optimism around new initiatives and Private Bank momentum. Analyst tone was similarly constructive, with some increased focus on execution risks and return timelines for announced programs.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Citizens provided significantly more detail on the Reimagine the Bank program in Q4, laying out specific cost, benefit, and timeline targets, whereas the previous quarter only hinted at its scope.

  • The outlook for loan and deposit growth was more assertive, with the Private Bank’s contributions and targets explicitly outlined for 2026 and beyond.

  • Operating leverage and efficiency ratio improvements are now more firmly quantified, with guidance for "operating leverage in excess of 500 basis points" and a mid-50s efficiency ratio medium-term target.

  • Analysts’ questions shifted toward the specifics of digital transformation, capital deployment, and Private Bank scalability compared to prior quarters’ broader strategic focus.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted execution risk around the Reimagine the Bank program, stating "we have a lot of wood to chop to actually execute this program."

  • Fee growth guidance was described as "cautious" given market and regulatory uncertainties.

  • Credit risk remains under watch, particularly in CRE, but Van Saun and Banerjee reported improving trends and robust allowance coverage.

  • Analysts flagged the sustainability of high Private Bank growth, the pace of deposit accumulation, and expense discipline as areas to monitor.

Final Takeaway

Citizens Financial Group closed 2025 with strong operating momentum, record Private Bank growth, and improved credit metrics. Management is projecting robust NII and loan growth for 2026, driven by continued Private Bank expansion, positive market conditions, and the large-scale Reimagine the Bank transformation. While the company expects significant operating leverage and capital returns, management emphasized disciplined execution and ongoing investments in strategic growth areas to sustain performance and achieve medium-term ROTCE targets.
